header_annotations.text: The "Bani Flow" panel opens with a raga (or composition). The chip above is {raga:reetigowla}, a janya of the 22nd mela {raga:kharaharapriya}. The ◈25 beside it opens the mela-family popup: the mela, 24 sibling janyas, and any Hindustani equivalents — each tappable to pivot the panel. For a composition subject, the counter shows the number of musicians who have recorded it; the popup shows the raga, tala, and composer.
The "Filter trail" input above narrows the sections below by composition name, musician name, or raga. The tree structure stays intact — sections collapse if empty.

sections.section_gloss: Lecture-demonstrations are how Carnatic musicians teach themselves to listeners. Before recordings, knowledge of a raga moved guru-to-shishya only; now it moves through these — and they remain the fastest doorway in.

data_refs.lecdem_label: How to Put Together a Goddess out of Musical Scales

rows.annotation: Meaning and context: {musician:david_shulman} is a Sanskritist and Musicologist ; his lecture reads {raga:reetigowla} as Dikshitar possibly read it — as a vessel for a goddess. Hear what the raga means (meant?) before you hear what it sounds like.

data_refs.lecdem_label: A Musical Exploration — Abhogi, Sriranjani, Ritigaula, Kanada and more

rows.annotation: Relationship to other ragas: {musician:seetha_rajan} sits {raga:reetigowla} beside its neighbours — {raga:abhogi}, {raga:sriranjani}, {raga:kaanada} — and shows how a swara of difference makes a world of difference. The clearest possible answer to 'how do these ragas, which share so many notes, not sound the same?'

Practice a.k.a how to improvise inside a raga: {musician:tm_krishna}'s classroom is the rasika's classroom too. Watch the alapana take shape phrase by phrase: this is improvisation as architecture, not ornament.

sections.section_gloss: A composition is the structured vessel through which a raga's personality becomes audible. Each renderer reveals a different face of the same mode — same notes, same words, different soul.

rows.annotation: The composition row carries — top to bottom — the composition ({comp:janani_ninnuvina}), its composer ({musician:subbaraya_sastri}), and the musicians who have recorded it in {raga:reetigowla}. The play button ▶ on a musician row launches that specific rendering. The composer chip is itself navigable — tap it to see everything Subbaraya Sastri ever wrote.

sections.section_gloss: Material that doesn't fit a composition — alapana sketches, Ragam-Tanam-Pallavis (RTPs), untitled fragments. The unsorted shelf of the tradition: often where the most uninhibited listening lives.

rows.annotation: An alapana is a raga thinking out loud — no composition, no tala, no destination. Hear {musician:md_ramanathan} move through {raga:reetigowla} in his unmistakable slow tempo: each swara dwelt in, the gamaka thick and patient. This is the raga as raga.

closing_note.text: Every play button ▶ opens the floating YouTube player. Tap {raga:kharaharapriya} to climb the {view:mela_janya} wheel — the wheel will rotate to centre the 22nd-mela swara-bucket and the ragas inside it become reachable in one tap. The tradition has two faces: this panel is the raga face, the {view:mela_janya} is the modal face, and they speak to each other through every chip you tap.

header_annotations.text: The chip above is {musician:ramnad_krishnan}. The colour encodes the era — six eras run from earliest to living: {era:trinity}, {era:bridge}, {era:golden_age}, {era:disseminator}, {era:living_pillars}, {era:contemporary}. Ramnad Krishnan (1918–1973) belongs to the {era:disseminator} generation: the artists who carried the bani out of the gurukula and onto record. The ↥4 is the sum of all guru-shishya relations and is in indication of connectedness in our database.
The "Filter recordings" input above narrows the sections below by raga, composition, or co-performer name. Sections collapse and reform around your query without losing their tree shape.

sections.section_gloss: Lectures about a musician — appreciations, oral histories, technical analyses of the artist's style. These appear at the top of the panel because a musician is best entered through someone who has thought hard about them.

rows.annotation: A lec-dem about {musician:ramnad_krishnan} hosted on {musician:savita_narasimhan}'s profile but surfaced here because Ramnad Krishnan is its subject. Lecdems are indexed by host and by subject, so they appear wherever their meaning lives. Begin a musician's profile with the lecdems — let someone introduce you before the recordings begin.

sections.section_gloss: Full-evening recordings, grouped by the night they happened. A concert is its own unit of attention — a single bow, an arc of pieces chosen for that hall on that day. Open a concert and listen the way the audience listened.

rows.annotation: Poonamallee 1965. Sung at Srinivasa Farms on the outskirts of Madras, on the occasion of Alathur Sivasubramania Iyer receiving the Sangita Kalanidhi. The chips below the title are the accompanying musicians — violin and mridangam — each itself a node in the graph: tap one to open that musician's own panel. The piece-count tells you how many performances are nested inside; the ▶ opens the entire concert as a setlist in the floating player.

Wesleyan 1967: Recorded at Wesleyan University in the United States with T. Viswanathan on flute — one of the first Carnatic concerts on a Western university stage. Co-performers whose chips appear muted are present in the recording metadata but not yet linked to a node in our graph: a curation invitation. Every concert is also an invitation to widen the lineage.

sections.section_gloss: Every individual rendering Ramnad Krishnan has on record, grouped first by raga, then by composition, then by version (different concerts, different years, different moods). Read it like a tree: the raga is the room, the composition is the door, the version is the way he walked through it that evening.

rows.annotation: {musician:ramnad_krishnan} in {raga:begada} is said to be unparalleled. {comp:abhimanamennedu} — a kriti of {musician:patnam_subramanya_iyer} — is one of the compositions he recorded in this raga. Tap ▶ to hear it; tap the composition chip to open {raga:begada}'s full Bani Flow trail across every musician in the graph who has recorded in it.

sections.section_gloss: This section lists compositions authored by this musician. {musician:ramnad_krishnan} was a performer of immense range but not a composer — so this section is empty. Open the panel of one of the Trinity instead — {musician:tyagaraja}, {musician:muthuswami_dikshitar}, or {musician:shyama_shastri} — and watch this section bloom into hundreds of works, each one a door into a raga.

closing_note.text: Every chip you tap in this panel echoes across the canvas. Tap a raga chip and the {view:mela_janya} wheel rotates to centre its swara-bucket. Tap a musician chip and the {view:guru_shishya} lights up the lineage — the chain of teachers and students through which this artist learnt and taught. The {view:guru_shishya} is sparse on purpose: only musicians connected by a documented relationship appear. The tradition is a forest, not a list.
